risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Block randomisation of nursing homes will use a computer generated allocation sequence and occur after initial consent. Randomisation will be stratified by organisational affiliation... [and] by rurality and dementia-specificity" Comment: This describes a proper random method (computer-generated allocation sequence) with block randomisation and stratification, which is sufficient to judge random sequence generation as low risk.

_evidence.method_quote: Block randomisation of nursing homes will use a computer generated allocation sequence and occur after initial consent. Randomisation will be stratified by organisational affiliation to control for the influence of organisational culture, policy and procedures on quality of care. Randomisation will also be stratified by rurality and dementia-specificity following evidence that these variables may be associated with quality of care for residents with advanced dementia [18].
_evidence.method_summary: Cluster randomisation of nursing homes using a computer-generated allocation sequence with block randomisation and stratification by organisational affiliation, rurality, and dementia-specificity.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Block randomisation of nursing homes will use a computer generated allocation sequence and occur after initial consent." Comment: This describes sequence generation and timing of randomisation, but it does not state any concealment mechanism that would prevent recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment. No mention is made of central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, sequentially numbered identical containers, or any other explicit concealment procedure, so allocation concealment remains unclear.

_evidence.concealment_quote: Block randomisation of nursing homes will use a computer generated allocation sequence and occur after initial consent.
_evidence.note: The article describes sequence generation and timing of randomisation, but does not describe how allocation was concealed from those enrolling nursing homes.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The pragmatic nature of the trial and complex nature of the intervention requires careful consideration of the feasibility versus desirability of blinding... However, nursing home managers will not be blinded to allocation... Nursing staff and families at nursing homes in the intervention arm will also know that facilitated case conferencing is being implemented because they will be participating... However, the lead investigator (MA), the national project coordinator (TL), and two state-based project managers will remain unblinded for the purpose of liaising with implementation personnel and ensuring protocol fidelity." Comment: Participants and key personnel were not fully blinded, and the study reports this explicitly. Because the trial includes multiple subjective outcomes (e.g. family- and nurse-rated comfort, symptom management, quality of life, satisfaction, attitudes, and interviews), knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence these outcomes, so the dominant risk for this domain is high.

_evidence.blinding_quote: “The pragmatic nature of the trial and complex nature of the intervention requires careful consideration of the feasibility versus desirability of blinding [43, 44]. The statistician (GL) will be blinded to nursing home identity for randomisation and analysis. However, nursing home managers will not be blinded to allocation because they need to make a fully informed decision regarding nursing home participation. Nursing staff and families at nursing homes in the intervention arm will also know that facilitated case conferencing is being implemented because they will be participating. ... However, the lead investigator (MA), the national project coordinator (TL), and two state-based project managers will remain unblinded for the purpose of liaising with implementation personnel and ensuring protocol fidelity.”
_evidence.blinding_method: “The design seeks to limit potential for bias by omitting from participant information any reference to the study’s evaluative aims or allocation of nursing homes to intervention versus usual care arms. Instead, verbal and written information will describe the study’s aim in more general terms as being concerned with understanding the factors associated with quality of care for residents with advanced dementia by comparing between nursing homes with different characteristics.”
outcome_categories: acute care episodes and length of stay
objective_outcomes: acute care episodes and length of stay

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Research assistants collecting process and outcome data will also be blinded to the specific design and aims of the study, and each assistant will visit nursing homes in either the intervention or usual care arm only." Comment: The study explicitly states that research assistants collecting outcome data were blinded, which supports low risk for outcome assessment. Although several outcomes were proxy- or family-rated (e.g. EOLD scales, QUALID, EQ-5D-5L, qPAD) and some were judged by expert review, the protocol indicates blinded outcome data collection and includes several objective record-based outcomes; this makes knowledge of allocation unlikely to influence outcome measurement overall.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: Research assistants collecting process and outcome data will also be blinded to the specific design and aims of the study, and each assistant will visit nursing homes in either the intervention or usual care arm only. ... All scales will be rated by families 4 to 6 weeks following resident death ... Nurse-rated symptom-related comfort and symptom management will be measured using the CAD-EOLD and SM-EOLD Scales and rated 4 weeks following residents’ death by the nurse who provides most care in the last 7 days and 90 days of life respectively. ... Proxy nurse-rated resident QOL will be measured second weekly using the Quality of Life in Late-stage Dementia (QUALID) Scale ... For the purposes of economic evaluation, QOL will also be assessed by proxy using the EQ-5D-5 L ... a palliative approach to care at the nursing home level will be measured using the following indices: rates of potentially inappropriate non-palliative interventions and acute care episodes and length of stay ... and number/type of complaints from families regarding the quality of care. ... Nursing home staff’s attitudes to, knowledge of and confidence in providing palliative/EOL care to residents with advanced dementia will be evaluated using the questionnaire on Palliative Care for Advanced Dementia (qPAD).

outcomes.label: potentially inappropriate non-palliative interventions and acute care episodes and length of stay
inappropriate acute care episodes and inappropriate non-palliative interventions as judged by an expert review
number/type of complaints from families regarding the quality of care

risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The article states only that "Primary and secondary analyses will be performed on an intention to treat (ITT) basis" and that a "dropout rate at the resident level of 10%" was anticipated for sample-size planning, but it provides no post-randomization attrition numbers, per-arm analysed denominators, CONSORT flow, or observed reasons for loss on the outcomes of interest. Comment: Because there are no actual reported attrition data for the analysed outcome, the paper does not let us judge whether missing data could have biased the results; therefore the appropriate rating is Unclear risk rather than High or Low.

_evidence.notes: Methods paper/protocol text describes planned analyses only; no post-randomisation attrition numbers or CONSORT-style flow were reported.
